policy_statement: Hiroshima City sets KPIs to increase introduced biomass boilers in city facilities to 3 by FY2020 and 9 by FY2030, and to increase area of park and green spaces to 1,000 ha by FY2020.
mechanism_description: The city tracks increasing installation of biomass boilers in its facilities to shift heat supply away from fossil fuels and reduce associated emissions.
primary_causal_pathway: Fossil heat demand → biomass boiler deployment → ↓ fossil fuel combustion → ↓ CO₂e emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Change in human/system activity: City facilities shift their heat generation technology and fuel from fossil-fired boilers (oil/gas/coal) to biomass boilers.  
(2) Mediating physical process: Reduced onsite combustion of fossil carbon; heat is produced via combustion of biogenic feedstocks (or biomass-derived fuels), with lifecycle emissions depending on feedstock sourcing, processing, and regrowth dynamics.  
(3) Climate-relevant outcome: Lower net CO₂e emissions from building/stationary energy use (and potentially lower upstream methane leakage if gas is displaced), contributing to mitigation.
dominant_pathway_test: Replacing fossil heating with biomass boilers reduces net GHG emissions from stationary heat generation by displacing fossil fuel combustion.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Fuel switching in heat supply leads to reduced fossil combustion and thus lower GHG emissions.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While there may be co-benefits (energy security, local air quality, possible waste utilization), the central climate effect is emissions reduction, not adaptation, resource efficiency (same service with less energy), or ecosystem-based risk reduction.  
3) Edge cases applied: If biomass is unsustainably sourced or has high lifecycle emissions, net mitigation may be smaller or negative; however, the mechanism as stated is explicitly intended to displace fossil heating to reduce emissions, so it remains a mitigation mechanism.  
4) Confirm applies to ALL instances sharing this mechanism: Any policy characterized by “biomass boiler deployment → fossil heating displacement” primarily acts through reducing fossil fuel combustion for heat, i.e., mitigation.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment deferred: Whether the policy is best tagged as renewable heat, municipal operations, district energy, waste-to-energy biomass, or includes sustainability safeguards requires policy-specific evidence and is deferred to policy-level coding.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Lifecycle accounting and feedstock sustainability can affect net GHG outcomes (including potential increases if biomass is high-carbon or causes land-use change). Air quality impacts and particulate controls may be material but are not the primary climate pathway. The representative policy text also mentions green space area, which is a separate nature-based mechanism and should not be conflated with the biomass boiler mechanism.
key_indicators: "Key Performance Indicator (KPI)"; "No. of introduced biomass boilers in the city facilities"; "cumulative total"; "3 (FY2020)"; "9 (FY2030)"; "Area of park and green spaces"; "1,000ha (FY2020)"
co_benefits: Potential air-quality/health impacts and energy resilience from biomass boiler deployment; potential urban heat island reduction, recreation/biodiversity, stormwater management from increased parks/green spaces.
instance_edge_case_notes: Single KPI table mixes mitigation (biomass boilers) with a nature/land-use KPI (parks/green spaces) that could be adaptation and/or mitigation (sequestration/urban cooling). With no accompanying implementation measures, classification is limited to target-setting rather than investment/procurement.
